one_line_takeaway: VERA turns black-box jailbreaking into amortized variational inference, training a LoRA attacker LLM to sample diverse, fluent, template-free jailbreak prompts in one shot, yielding SOTA black-box ASR (50.5% avg over 8 models) plus strong diversity, transferability, and defense-robustness.
key_finding: On HarmBench, VERA reaches state-of-the-art black-box ASR and beats even white-box baselines on 5 of 7 open-source targets, with an average ASR of 50.5% across 8 models. It attains 70.0% on Vicuna-7B, 72.0% on Orca2-7B, 64.8% on Baichuan2-7B, 63.5% on R2D2 (Zephyr-robust), 53.3% on GPT-3.5, and 48.5% on Gemini-Pro, trailing only on the strongly RLHF-aligned LLaMA2 family (10.8% / 21.0%). Beyond raw ASR, VERA's distinctive wins are diversity (self-BLEU 0.420 vs 0.788-0.924 for baselines; BLEU-to-template 0.003 vs 0.241-0.649), template independence, amortized scalable generation (>5x more successful attacks than GPTFuzzer and ~2.5x more than AutoDAN under a fixed 1250s time budget), strong cross-model transferability (e.g., GPT-3.5-crafted prompts hit 78.9% on Vicuna, 60.9% on Baichuan2), and superior robustness to defenses (58.9% ASR under Perplexity Filter vs 48.8/21.5 for AutoDAN/GCG; 9.6% under Circuit Breaker where AutoDAN and GCG drop to 0.0%).
core_contribution: VERA (Variational infErence fRamework for jAilbreaking) reframes black-box LLM jailbreaking as a variational inference / posterior-inference problem rather than per-prompt genetic/search optimization. A small pretrained attacker LLM (fine-tuned with LoRA adaptors) is trained as a variational distribution q_theta(x) to approximate the target model's posterior over adversarial prompts P_LM(x | y in Yharm). Once trained on a target behavior, the attacker samples diverse, fluent jailbreak prompts in a single forward pass with no test-time re-optimization or search, and without bootstrapping from human-written jailbreak templates. This is the paper's OWN contribution; genetic algorithms (GPTFuzzer, AutoDAN), search/LLM-guided methods (PAIR, TAP), and RL-based attackers are cited prior work it contrasts against.
threat_model: Fully black-box adversary with API-only access to the target LLM (no gradients, no logits, no internal access), reflecting real deployment. The adversary can query the target and observe outputs, and uses an external black-box judge model that maps (prompt, response) to a harmfulness score in [0,1]. The attacker is a separate small open-source LLM the adversary controls and fine-tunes. Goal is red-teaming: eliciting harmful responses that comply with a specified malicious behavior, and characterizing the breadth of a model's vulnerability for a given behavior rather than finding isolated single successes.
limitations: Weak against strong RLHF-aligned deterministic safety filters: only 10.8% on LLaMA2-7B and 21.0% on LLaMA2-13B, where white-box GCG exploiting internals does better. The distributional/comprehensive-coverage benefit inherently requires a much higher volume of sequential black-box target queries, which the authors concede is a necessary cost that does not parallelize on APIs. There is an upfront per-behavior attacker training cost (amortized only when generating many attacks). AutoDAN-Turbo was excluded because the authors could not reproduce it. Circuit Breaker was only evaluated in the LLaMA3-8B-Instruct configuration (official repo supports only LLaMA3-8B and Mistral). Effectiveness depends on judge-model quality, which serves as a proxy for the true harmful-likelihood. Minor internal inconsistency: the conclusion cites 'up to 78.6%' ASR, but the highest main-table (Table 1) value is 72.0% (Orca2-7B); the ~78-79% figure comes from the transfer setting (GPT-3.5 -> Vicuna, 78.9%).

datasets_used: HarmBench (400 harmful behaviors across 7 categories; primary benchmark)
HarmBench 50-behavior subset (for diversity/novelty/scalability study against Vicuna-7B)
quantitative_results: VERA HarmBench ASR: Vicuna-7B 70.0%, Orca2-7B 72.0%, Baichuan2-7B 64.8%, R2D2 63.5%, GPT-3.5 53.3%, Gemini-Pro 48.5%, LLaMA2-7B 10.8%, LLaMA2-13B 21.0%; average 50.5% (Table 1, best average of all methods)
Diversity self-BLEU (lower=more diverse): VERA 0.420 vs GPTFuzzer 0.846, GPTFuzzer* 0.924, AutoDAN 0.788, AutoDAN* 0.709
BLEU-to-template / novelty (lower=less template-dependent): VERA 0.003 vs GPTFuzzer 0.450, GPTFuzzer* 0.337, AutoDAN 0.649, AutoDAN* 0.241
Fixed 1250s time budget: VERA >5x more successful attacks than either GPTFuzzer variant and ~2.5x more than either AutoDAN variant
Transferability ASR: LLaMA2-7B-crafted -> Vicuna 62.8%, Baichuan2 55.8%, R2D2 55.8%; GPT-3.5-crafted -> Vicuna 78.9%, Baichuan2 60.9%
Under Perplexity Filter (Vicuna-7B): VERA 58.9% (from 70.0%), AutoDAN 48.8% (from 66.0%), GCG 21.5% (from 65.5%)
Under Circuit Breaker (LLaMA3-8B-Instruct): VERA 9.6% (from 38.3%), AutoDAN 0.0% (from 12.8%), GCG 0.0% (from 34.0%)
baselines_compared: White-box: GCG, GCG-M, GCG-T, PEZ, GBDA, UAT, AP
Black-box: SFS, ZS, PAIR, TAP, TAP-T, AutoDAN, PAP-top5, Human, Direct
Diversity/scalability study: GPTFuzzer and AutoDAN (plus asterisked variants restricted to templates that cannot jailbreak on their own)
Defenses tested against: Perplexity Filter, Circuit Breaker (main text); LLaMA Guard, SmoothLLM, RA-LLM (Appendix C)
